name: Nutrient Detective Food Sort
description: Learners examine a variety of foods (pictures or actual items with safety considerations) and sort them by nutrient type: proteins, carbohydrates, fats, and water-rich foods. Uses color coding, handling foods when safe, and discussing why variety matters. Develops observational skills and introduces nutrient categories in a concrete, sensory way.
materials: Food pictures or safe food samples
learning_objectives: Recognize that different foods contain different nutrients
Sort foods by primary nutrient type
Understand that most foods contain multiple nutrients
Appreciate variety in foods
variations: Use only pictures if food allergies are a concern
Expand to micronutrient categories (vitamin C foods, calcium foods, etc.)
Older learners: read nutrition labels to verify their sorting

name: Vitamin & Mineral Bingo
description: Bingo game where each square contains a vitamin or mineral and its function (e.g., 'Calcium - builds strong bones'). Caller reads function descriptions; learners find matching nutrient on their card. Combines game fun with learning micronutrient functions. Can be adapted for complexity.
materials: Sample foods rich in each nutrient
learning_objectives: Name key vitamins and minerals
Describe the role of each micronutrient in the body
Connect micronutrients to food sources
Recognize importance of variety
variations: Advanced: include actual nutrient names (calciferol for Vitamin D) for older learners
Foods version: caller says function, learners match to foods that contain it
Crossover: connect to digestion or health integration modules

name: Digestion Journey Map
description: Learners trace the path of a specific food (e.g., a peanut butter sandwich) through the digestive system. They draw or map the journey with stations: mouth (mechanical + chemical breakdown), stomach (churning, acid), small intestine (major absorption), large intestine (water absorption, elimination). At each station, they note what happens and which nutrients are affected. Can include time estimates or enzyme demonstrations.
materials: Scientific labels for organs and enzymes
learning_objectives: Trace the path of food through digestive organs in order
Describe what happens at each stage of digestion
Understand the role of enzymes in breakdown
Recognize that different nutrients are processed differently
variations: Follow different foods: banana vs. cheese vs. chicken for different nutrient types
Time-based: add timestamps for how long food spends at each stage
Interactive: learners physically move through a life-sized digestive system setup

name: Enzyme Experiment with Crackers
description: Learners place a plain cracker in their mouth without chewing and observe how the taste changes as saliva (containing amylase enzyme) breaks down starch into sugar. They record observations: sweet taste develops, texture changes, time taken. Discusses how mechanical and chemical digestion happen simultaneously. Concrete demonstration of enzyme action at work in their own mouths.
materials: Optional: iodine solution to test for starch before/after
learning_objectives: Observe enzyme action in real time
Understand that chemical breakdown starts in the mouth
Recognize that starches become simpler sugars through enzyme action
Appreciate the role of saliva in digestion
variations: Younger learners: focus on taste change observation only
Older learners: add iodine test (blue-black with starch, clear with sugars) to show chemical change
Protein variation: enzyme experiment with gelatin and pineapple juice (bromelain enzyme)

name: Farm-to-Table Mapping
description: Learners choose a food they like and trace its journey from farm/source to their table. They research: Where is it grown? How is it transported? Who handles it? What's involved in each step? They create a visual map or infographic showing the journey, key people/processes, and environmental/social considerations. Develops systems thinking and food literacy.
materials: Research resources (books, websites, local farmer interviews)
Poster paper or digital design tool
learning_objectives: Trace a food from source to consumption
Understand the complexity of food systems
Recognize people and labor in food production
Consider environmental and social impacts
variations: Local focus: research a food from a nearby farm or producer
Comparison: trace two versions of same food (organic vs. conventional, local vs. imported)
Interview: if possible, visit a farm or interview a farmer/grocer to add real voices

name: Food Miles Calculator
description: Using a food miles calculator (online or paper-based) and a map, learners calculate how far their food travels from farm to table. They explore: produce from different countries, seasons, and farming methods. They discuss trade-offs: local strawberries in season vs. imported strawberries year-round. Creates awareness of environmental impact of food choices without judgment.
materials: Nutrition labels showing country of origin
learning_objectives: Calculate distance food travels
Understand transportation as environmental impact
Recognize seasonal variation and availability
Discuss trade-offs in food choices
variations: Younger learners: focus on near/far concept with maps
Carbon footprint: extend to calculate estimated emissions from transportation
Comparative analysis: create charts showing miles for different foods or sources

name: Nutrition Label Scavenger Hunt
description: Learners search real nutrition labels (from home, school, or printed examples) to answer specific questions: How much protein? What's the ingredient list? Where's the fiber? Are there added sugars? Teaches label literacy in an engaging, game-like format. Can include comparing similar foods to notice differences.
materials: Real nutrition labels or printed examples
learning_objectives: Locate key information on nutrition facts labels
Interpret numbers and percentages
Read ingredient lists
Compare similar foods using labels
variations: Speed challenge: first to find the answer wins
Comparison: hunt for same food from different brands and compare
Advanced: calculate total carbohydrates from individual sources, interpret % Daily Value

name: Claim vs. Evidence Analysis
description: Learners receive real nutrition claims from products, social media, or advertising (e.g., 'This supplement boosts immunity', 'Organic is always healthier'). They research to find (or fail to find) scientific evidence. They evaluate: Is there a study? Who paid for it? How big was the study? What do independent reviews say? Develops critical thinking and media literacy.
materials: Access to databases like PubMed if available
learning_objectives: Distinguish between claims and evidence
Research the support (or lack thereof) for claims
Identify potential conflicts of interest
Develop healthy skepticism toward marketing
Communicate findings clearly
variations: Younger learners: simple claims with clear evidence or lack thereof
Debate: divide into pro/con teams and argue based on evidence
Create your own: learners write a nutrition claim and explain what evidence would be needed

name: Balanced Meal Design Challenge
description: Learners design a meal (breakfast, lunch, dinner, or snack) that includes protein, carbohydrates, fat, and vegetables/fruits. They can use food pictures, menu planning tools, or actual cooking. Can include constraints (budget, allergies, preferences, available ingredients) to make it realistic. Teaches practical application of nutrition knowledge without restriction or judgment.
learning_objectives: Create balanced meals including multiple food groups
Consider nutritional variety in food choices
Apply knowledge of nutrients to real meals
Adapt meals for different preferences and constraints
variations: Culture-specific: design meals featuring cuisines from learners' cultures
Budget constraint: design nutritious meals with limited budget
Allergy-friendly: design meals avoiding specific allergens
Cooking: if possible, actually prepare and eat one designed meal together

name: Weekly Meal Planning Workshop
description: Learners plan meals for a week, considering nutritional balance, budget, time, preferences, and family needs. If possible, coordinate with families to use this as actual planning for real meals. Includes grocery list creation and consideration of food preparation time. Teaches practical life skills: nutrition, budgeting, time management, cooking.
materials: Grocery store ads or price lists
learning_objectives: Plan nutritionally balanced meals for multiple days
Consider budget and food access
Account for time and cooking skills available
Incorporate personal/family preferences and cultural foods
Create efficient grocery lists
variations: Family involvement: learners conduct interviews about family food preferences, budget, and time
Shopping trip: learners purchase ingredients and prepare one planned meal together
Restaurant menu planning: learners plan balanced meals when eating out
Cooking together: learners prepare one or more meals from their plan

name: Sleep and Nutrition Connection Exploration
description: Learners explore how nutrition affects sleep and sleep affects food choices. Topics: foods that promote sleep (tryptophan, magnesium), caffeine timing, meals close to bedtime, impact of poor sleep on hunger hormones and food cravings. They track their own sleep and food for several days, noting patterns (if willing and comfortable). Discussion of how to support good sleep through nutrition.
materials: Research articles on sleep and nutrition
learning_objectives: Understand how sleep quality affects eating patterns
Recognize foods and timing that support sleep
Appreciate the bi-directional relationship between nutrition and sleep
Apply understanding to personal choices
variations: Younger learners: focus on simple connections (heavy meals before bed make sleep hard)
Tracking: optional personal data collection and pattern identification
Science focus: discuss hormones (melatonin, ghrelin, leptin) involved in sleep and hunger

name: Stress and Eating Discussion and Reflection
description: Learners discuss how stress affects eating: emotional eating, stress-induced appetite loss, comfort foods, cravings under pressure. They explore mindfulness strategies, stress management through food (nutrition supporting stress resilience), and self-compassion around food choices under stress. Avoids judgment; centers on understanding and support. May include personal reflection journaling.
learning_objectives: Recognize connections between stress and eating
Understand physiological responses to stress
Develop self-compassion around food choices under stress
Explore stress-management strategies including nutrition
variations: Younger learners: focus on recognizing stress and simple coping strategies
Older learners: discuss hunger hormones (cortisol) and physiological impacts of chronic stress
Action-focused: develop personal stress-management plan including nutrition, movement, sleep
